journal-title-group.journal-title: The Yale Journal of Biology and Medicine

publisher.publisher-name: Yale Journal of Biology and Medicine

title-group.article-title: “In-Flu-Enza and Out-Flew Hair:” Post-Epidemic Health and the Importance of the History of Epidemics

contrib-group.aff: Affiliate Faculty, Health & Medical Humanities Program, University of North Carolina, Charlotte, NC, USA

author-notes.corresp: *To whom all correspondence should be addressed: Bethany L. Johnson, bethanyjohnson@uncc.edu; ORCID: https://orcid.org/0000-0003-1949-041X.

permissions.copyright-statement: Copyright ©2025, Yale Journal of Biology and Medicine
permissions.copyright-holder: Yale Journal of Biology and Medicine

license.license-p: This is an open access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons CC BY-NC license, which permits use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited. You may not use the material for commercial purposes.

abstract.p: When COVID-19 survivors reported ongoing symptoms or new health concerns following their infections in 2020 and early 2021, many medical practitioners and health agencies questioned the connection between novel viruses and long-term health impacts. Medical historians studying epidemics understand the connection between viral infection and health complications emerging immediately or years or decades later. In this essay, I explore the similarities between the medical fallout of the 1918 influenza and COVID-19 pandemics. Despite the differences between the viruses, these novel strains produced similar medium- and long-term health difficulties, including cardiovascular dysfunction and crushing fatigue. As I demonstrate, a significant difference between these two pandemics is in the response by medical practitioners. Following influenza, practitioners expected new and worsening health issues and took their patients’ complaints seriously, offering support through food delivery, convalescent care, specialist oversight, and in-home nursing. Early in the COVID-19 pandemic, many practitioners characterized ongoing or new symptoms as anxiety. Patients led efforts to recognize Long COVID as an authentic medical condition, and today, physicians around the country refer their patients to Long COVID clinics. The value of medical history is apparent in this comparison—if practitioners understand how historical epidemics impacted various populations, they expect that in the epidemic aftermath or the period following an acute epidemic crisis, not all patients get well. Including the history of epidemics in public health education, continuing education programming, and even medical school curricula can resist epidemic erasure and empower medical practitioners to expect the unexpected.

funding-group.funding-statement: Funding for this research was provided by the Consortium for History of Science, Technology and Medicine, Philadelphia, PA.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: In January 2020, as the COVID-19 pandemic drew international attention, Americans watched the 2011 movie Contagion, pushing the film to No. 10 on the “iTunes Top Movie List” [1]. In the film, the character Beth Emhoff contracts a flu-like illness on a business trip abroad and dies two days later in the United States. The disease becomes a pandemic, and the film traces the efforts of various physicians and institutions, like the CDC, to isolate the virus and create a vaccine, even as public panic results in widespread social disorder. At the end of the film, the US government distributes an effective vaccine via lottery, and those with vaccine bracelets can move about freely. Like me, millions of Americans viewed this and other familiar imaginaries of a pandemic world (eg, Outbreak, 1995) to consider how a not-yet-here pandemic might reshape our lives.1 As COVID-19 exploded in the US and subsequent waves continued to complicate daily life with quarantines, social distancing, and shifting masking regulations, it became even more evident that epidemics do not end as they do in movies. Instead, they linger as individuals and communities contend with epidemic fallout, including the health impacts experienced by survivors.

As a historian of medicine and epidemics, living through a global pandemic deepened my certainty that studying historical epidemics has value in the present—for individuals, communities, and institutions, including medical schools. While plague historians were long engaged with disease history, Rosenberg’s 1968 The Cholera Years helped catalyze focused attention on modern epidemics within American historical scholarship.2 In 1976, Alfred Crosby explored the 1918 influenza epidemic in Epidemic and Peace 1918, tracing the disease through the US with a close study of Philadelphia and San Francisco. Crosby considered how politicians, public health systems, and local inhabitants responded to the outbreak, and his incisive work encouraged generations of historians to study influenza.3 Today, medical history is a diverse and growing field, including the work of historians of epidemics and pandemics, and, recently, epidemic ends [2-4]. Epidemic historians publish in diverse journals, including the Yale Journal of Biology and Medicine. In 2020, the American Historical Review featured Monica Green’s “The Four Black Deaths,” highlighting the utility of paleogenetics in historical epidemic analysis and the importance of studying plague beyond Europe [5].

Studying historical epidemics allows scholars to observe communities, regions, and nations under threat from an external force, ostensibly with the power to sicken or kill individuals of any rank and from any class or ethnic group. Often recounted as events with discernable temporal boundaries, studies of epidemics generally begin just before an outbreak, follow the epidemic to its zenith, discuss the days or weeks following the worst of the crisis, and conclude with an overview of the impact years later (eg, the rise of flu season). This traditional narrative arc provides insights into disease epidemiology, social responses to public health crises, and the intersections between local and national politics and individual health. This structure, termed “dramaturgical” by epidemic historian Charles Rosenberg, depicts epidemics as biological and social phenomena in which communities “act out proprietary rituals that incorporate and reaffirm fundamental social values and modes of understanding” [6]. How epidemics interrupt social hierarchies or affirm social values is explored, in part, by examining how societies respond to the needs of victims and survivors.

When the novel virus COVID-19 entered the grand drama of human biology, media sources sought historians who could describe how societies responded to global pandemics in the past. Newspapers such as The New York Times published pieces on the 1918 influenza outbreak, including the epidemic in Philadelphia [7]. As COVID-19 spread and communities worldwide went into lockdown, scholars interested in the questions posed by epidemics and their outcomes reconsidered how and when epidemics end. Are epidemic ends defined by dropping case rates and hospitalizations, the end of political recognition, the cessation of large-scale public health response, individual behavioral adjustment, some combination of these, or something else altogether [2-4]?

In 2022, a special issue of Centaurus edited by historian Erica Charters titled “How Epidemics End” offered theories on epidemic ends from mathematicians, geographers, bioarcheologists, and historians, among others. Historian Virginia Berridge distinguished between the medical end of epidemics, marked by case drop-off following the introduction of medical technologies, like vaccines or pharmaceutical treatments, the definitional end when the acute epidemic crisis is over and the disease “assimilates into dominant trends in contemporary public health,” and the political end, when an epidemic becomes politically neutral among the voting public [3]. The work of Erica Charters, Virginia Berridge, Einar Wigen, and other historians encouraged me to reconsider the dramaturgical arc of the 1918 influenza outbreak.

Most influenza scholarship highlights the precipitous drop-off of cases at the end of the fall 1918 wave. However, as COVID-19 demonstrated, survivors can experience unexpected health problems in the wake of infection. My earlier interest in epidemics shifted toward epidemic ends, particularly as I read new media calling the influenza of 1918 “forgotten” [7]. I focused on Philadelphia—one of the hardest-hit cities in America—as a site for the study of epidemic ends because the high morbidity rate offered me a path into the aftermath.4 As I mined untapped archival resources, including the student and institutional records of Girard College and the papers of the Emergency Aids of Pennsylvania, I found surprising overlaps between the health problems and chronic illnesses that followed influenza in 1918 and COVID-19 in 2020 and 2021, despite the etiological differences between the diseases. Familiarity with these overlaps demonstrates the utility of epidemic history as a resource for reconsidering how and when epidemics end, including for survivors who struggle to get well.

I also observed that the response from medical practitioners varied significantly between the two pandemics, particularly the post-infection response. In the early 20th century, when epidemic disease still took many lives each year, physicians understood that a novel disease could cause unusual health problems in survivors. Early in the COVID-19 pandemic, many medical practitioners resisted the notion that survivors could remain unwell or become sicker, in part because many were unfamiliar with the long history of viral infection and latent health complications. Long-term viral conditions like post-polio syndrome may be unfamiliar to doctors trained in the last two decades; some will never meet a polio survivor, much less treat the late onset of symptoms. Conversely, pediatricians are familiar with subacute sclerosing panencephalitis (SSPE), a condition that causes progressive brain inflammation and is nearly always fatal. While SSPE kills infants in America each year, the public is likely unaware that among measles survivors, 1:1367 children under 5 years and 1:609 children under 12 months at the time of infection will experience SSPE [8]. The disease has a latency period of about 9 years, meaning SSPE does not afflict survivors until they are around 12-years-old [8]. A 2016 study concluded, “SSPE demonstrates the high human cost of ‘natural’ measles immunity” [8]. If we do not understand the medical implications of epidemic survival, we cannot communicate the complications of surviving infectious diseases to the public. This is particularly important today, as local, state, and federal policy changes could prompt the return of widespread outbreaks of tuberculosis, measles, and other preventable diseases.

As history demonstrates, the medical fallout from a novel viral strain in humans can create all kinds of health complications, even among healthy adults who experienced light cases. Physicians, nurses, and other medical practitioners discussed the health challenges following influenza in detail in the months following the fall 1918 influenza outbreak. Many feared the “wicked malady” influenza permanently harmed their patients, even though they were unsure what said malady was [9]. By exploring the overlapping sequelae of the 1918 influenza and COVID-19, we begin to see that our expectations for epidemic ends do not reflect the reality of survivors whose lives are permanently altered by viral infection.

sec.title: Similarities: Hair Loss and Other Post-Infection Health Problems
sec.p: On Sunday, August 3, 1919, an advertisement in the Richmond Times-Dispatch displayed a young woman with long, lush, wavy tresses smiling down at a dog beneath the phrase “In-Flu-Enza and Out-Flew Hair” [10]5. This advertisement for Sergeant’s Mange Medicine echoed a story familiar to many Americans that summer, as many survivors of the fall 1918 influenza wave grappled with severe debility, chronic disease, and prolonged symptoms like coughing, fatigue, and loss of appetite. The advertisement stated that the outbreak “left most people in a run down [sic] condition; some developed rheumatism, a few got deaf; but the greatest malady caused mental anguish rather than physical pain. It was the loss of hair [10].” Author and historian Francis Russell, who was a third grader in Dorchester, Massachusetts, during the 1918 outbreak, recalled a rhyme that students sang while jumping rope at recess:
And in-flew-Enza [11,12]

The short poem became enough of a cultural touchpoint that nearly a year later, the purveyors of Sergeant’s Mange Medicine knew readers were in on the joke, as it were. But how many readers were still waiting for their hair to return? How many others continued to struggle with other influenza-related health problems? As my research on the influenza aftermath in Philadelphia suggests, many thousands of Americans struggled with ongoing ill health and chronic illness well into 1919 and beyond.

Hair loss is one of the many symptoms 17-year-old survivor Grace Meyercord faced after her near-death brush with influenza. After becoming ill in October 1918, her high fever turned her hair white and then it fell out in clumps [13]. Author Katherine Anne Porter described her hair loss in several interviews, and the story appears in at least two biographies. Givner [14] reported that during Porter’s bout with influenza, a dangerously high fever caused her dark hair to turn white and fall out; Unrue [15] stated that her hair also grew back white. In Porter’s novella Pale Horse Pale Rider (1939), which explored the influenza outbreak in Colorado, the character Miranda Gay described her unusual symptoms: “I can’t smell or see or hear today. I must have a fearful cold [16].” During the COVID-19 waves in 2020 and early 2021, loss of smell or taste was also an early indicator of an infection.

In early 2020, Americans did not just watch epidemic films. They also flocked to social media—especially the new platform TikTok—sharing sourdough starters, family dance routines, and animal antics to escape the loneliness and uncertainty of lockdown. Alongside these posts, social media users asked questions about their post-COVID experience, including the experience of hair loss. Dermatologists posted videos on YouTube explaining the phenomenon [17]. Researchers in Saudi Arabia found that 52% of their study participants (n=806) experienced hair loss after a COVID-19 infection, particularly those who experienced a high fever [18]. As dermatologists on TikTok and YouTube shared, this type of hair loss, known as telogen effluvium, occurs when the hair growth cycle shifts 3 to 4 months after a stressful health event like a viral infection, a high fever, or a severe life stressor.6 Most COVID-19 survivors’ hair returned over time, just as it did for influenza survivors. Interestingly, temporary and permanent sensory loss and hair loss are not the only similarities between the health aftermath of COVID-19 and influenza.

Each epidemic imprints vestiges of itself in survivors and their communities, whether physical, social, economic or all of these. Physical changes like scars (eg, smallpox) or paralysis (eg, polio) are readily apparent after some outbreaks. Respiratory illnesses, like influenza and COVID-19, affect the body internally, making their impact less visible. Often, the echoes of respiratory epidemics are heard rather than seen—survivors struggling for breath after the 1918 influenza pandemic or the irregular, rapid heartbeat of some COVID-19 survivors heard through a stethoscope [19,20]. The experience of the COVID-19 infection lives on in some, causing dizziness, memory impairment, extreme fatigue, depression, and deep, unrelenting pain, symptoms which are also difficult to see. Similarly, after influenza, survivors faced lingering symptoms such as limb weakness, exhaustion, cardiac dysfunction, cognitive difficulties, headaches, and what was then termed “poor mental hygiene,” or what today we call mental illness.

The physical fallout from the 1918 influenza was severe and varied. Institutions caring for orphaned children described pediatric survivors with hair loss, hearing issues, strabismus, impaired heart function, post-influenzal paralysis, and even “cases of insanity” [21,22]. Among adults, doctors reported post-influenzal tuberculosis, nephritis (ie, kidney disease), and a lengthy recovery period during which some patients were too weak to work [23]. The director of the Visiting Nurses Society in Philadelphia stated that ‘recovered’ is not a word that can be generally applied…people apparently recover, though often without regaining normal vigor, and then weeks later a serious malady directly traceable appears [24].”

Well-known Philadelphian surgeon John Deaver noted that the “insidious disease” affected nearly every organ and bodily system and, thus, the body’s resistance to other diseases or complications, including neuralgias, phlebitis, and parotitis (ie, mumps) [25]. Some physicians believed that a global pandemic of what was called encephalitis lethargica (EL) emerged because of a widespread, lowered resistance to disease among survivors [26]. EL causes brain swelling, heavy somnolence, and sometimes coma and paralysis. In 1919, Philadelphia’s Department of Public Health and Charities made EL a reportable disease. Philadelphia neurologist Dr. Charles Frazier treated post-influenza headaches, brain fog, and other neurological conditions as part of his treatments for EL. Patient intakes asked about past influenza infections to help Frazier assess neurological dysfunction and possible encephalitic inflammation [27]. Whether or not these diseases were related, medical practitioners in Philadelphia were confident that neurological dysfunction and infections like EL could and did follow influenza survival.

Beginning early in the COVID-19 pandemic, some patients did not improve after getting a negative test result or they developed new symptoms months after their infection. In the edited collection The Long Covid Reader written by self-identified “long haulers,” patients like Vanessa Svihla described months of chest pain, fatigue, tachycardia, brain fog, headaches, and dizziness [28].7 During Ellen MacKenzie’s second COVID-19 infection, she developed hypertension and suffered a heart attack. Eighteen months later, she continued to struggle with high blood pressure, fatigue, cognitive challenges, and a lost sense of taste [28]. Judith Rosenbaum shared that her daily experiences vary: “It’s the uncertainty that gets me,” she wrote. “I reach tentatively into my limbs to take stock. Will this be a good day or a bad day [28]?” Brian Burke, who fell ill in early 2021, shared that he rarely ventures beyond the local library or neighborhood grocery store, as even short trips leave him drained. “I have to lie down when I get home—if I even make it home before collapsing,” he says [28].

Influenza and COVID-19 come from different virus families—Orthomyxoviridae and Coronaviridae, respectively— and spread through the body in distinct ways. Nevertheless, survivors of novel virus strains in 1918 and 2020 reported some of the same health struggles in the aftermath, including weakness and fatigue, cardiac conditions, brain fog, hair loss, and lowered resistance to other diseases. Yet the experience of influenza and COVID-19 survivors diverged in their pursuit of medical care tailored to post-viral medical issues. In an oral history interview, Rowan Reyes recounted surviving COVID twice, only to spend 2 years searching for a cardiologist who took their sudden, erratic heartbeats and frequent lightheadedness seriously [20,29]. The process of seeking medical support differed because many doctors in 2020 lacked sufficient knowledge of epidemic history and did not anticipate patients struggling with lingering or new health issues following their infections.

sec.title: Differences: Seeking Medical Support and Formal Diagnosis
sec.p: In 1918, visiting nurses, hospital physicians, and specialists observed the ongoing health struggles of survivors as a standard—even expected—consequence of viral infection. Despite the assumption that some patients would experience lasting health conditions, doctors remained humbled by their lack of knowledge—practitioners were not sure if influenza was bacterial, filterable (viral), or something else as yet undiscovered. Physicians admitted that the scientific advancements and life-saving interventions effective for other epidemics (eg, diphtheria) proved irrelevant for the influenza of 1918. Despite their inability to treat flu cases effectively, medical practitioners in Philadelphia understood that patients needed care and support via daily nursing, special diets, extended rest, and referrals to specialists like the neurologist Dr. Charles Frazier, or the experts at the Henry Phipps Institute, who treated tuberculosis. Although the extent of medical discoveries limited treatments at the time—neither antibiotic nor steroidal treatments were available—doctors supported patients using the full range of available treatments including convalescent care, extended rest, and nutritional support to aid the return of vitality and health.

Follow-up care at major institutions like the Philadelphia General Hospital continued through 1919. A doctor at Philadelphia General shared that in the year after the fall influenza wave, medical practitioners observed “many manifestations of the aftermath. Here appeared a case of tuberculosis, unmistakably traceable to a previous acute influenzal infection, and there a toxic or other psychosis, appearing days, or even weeks after…influenza had disappeared [30].” The Visiting Nurses Society of Philadelphia continued to raise funds for survivor care, up to and including the payment of rental bills and the provision of food, which provided ailing survivors additional time to convalesce with the oversight of medical providers.

Unfortunately, many COVID-19 survivors did not receive this kind of support from their medical practitioners, particularly between the spring of 2020 and October of 2021. More than a dozen doctors dismissed Candice Johnson’s account of the unusual symptoms that followed her COVID-19 infection [28]. When Dr. Lee Bar-Eli didn’t fully recover after her COVID-19 infection, she faced an uncomfortable change in perspective. “I not only became a patient,” she reflects, “but a patient whose symptoms weren’t always taken seriously. Experiencing that firsthand was incredibly painful [31].” Many patients experienced medical professionals dismissing their symptoms or blaming them on anxiety. In The Long Covid Reader, patient John Zeising argued that some medical practitioners held an “unsaid (or stated) presumption that our physical symptoms are manifestations of depression or anxiety [28].”8 According to one review of Long COVID literature, doctors can mistake dysautonomia, or dysregulation in the autonomic nervous system, for a psychiatric or psychological condition [32]. The authors note that “anxiety scales often include autonomic symptoms such as tachycardia and depression scales include symptoms such as fatigue, “resulting in misdiagnosis and frustration for Long COVID patients and medical providers [32].” And of course, not being believed can prompt both anxiety and depression.

Early in the COVID-19 pandemic (2020-2021), online communities took seriously the reality of complicated health after a COVID-19 infection. Within these groups, folks began to refer to themselves as “long-haulers” [28]. Patient-led advocacy groups formed and campaigned through online networks for over a year before leading health organizations formally recognized Long COVID [33]. By mid-2022, many physicians acknowledged Long COVID, and the ongoing scientific effort to understand the potential long-term impacts of COVID-19 infection resulted in thousands of scientific publications [33]. Self-described physician scientist Dr. Ziyah Al-Aly testified before the United States Senate on Long COVID. In July 2024, Al-Aly noted that “COVID-19 can still wreak havoc in many organs years after the initial viral infection [34].” While millions of Long COVID patients have seen an improvement in symptoms or regained the health they lost in the aftermath of their infection, many millions remain debilitated or disabled, even bedridden. Experts place this number at over 65 million people worldwide [32,34].

Like influenza survivors with lingering symptoms and new health conditions, many COVID-19 survivors face limited treatment options. However, their medical outlook is comparatively better. Scientists rapidly sequenced COVID-19 strains early in the pandemic, and pharmaceutical companies developed new vaccines and antivirals like Paxlovid. In contrast, influenza researchers struggled for over a decade to identify the virus. Today, there is still no scientific consensus on what caused the virulence of the 1918 strain or the disease’s diverse post-viral sequalae.

sec.title: Conclusions and Outlook
sec.p: My study of the aftermath of influenza in Philadelphia and my experience living through a global pandemic shifted my perspective on how epidemics end. The epidemic aftermath, or the period following an epidemic crisis when cases are highest, varies in length, depending on the community in question. For survivors facing ongoing or new symptoms post-infection, the end of an epidemic is not obvious; some suffering from Long COVID still await the end of their epidemic experience. Wigen observes that “it becomes almost impossible to distinguish between the life and the afterlife” of an outbreak [4, p.280]. While President Biden announced that the pandemic was “over” in September 2022, the federal health emergency status and related resource provision lasted until April 2023. So, when did the acute phase of COVID-19 end? And how does an ongoing increase in Long COVID cases complicate how we understand and define the end of the COVID-19 pandemic? According to Dr. Al-Aly, the rate at which new Long COVID patients received a diagnosis in 2024 is on par with rates in 2022 [34]. While the development of Long COVID cases has slowed significantly since late 2020, particularly among the vaccinated, Long COVID remains a risk because of repeat infections.

Although the 1918 pandemic is often described as “forgotten,” scholars such as Guy Beiner argue that the 1918 pandemic is unevenly remembered; survivor experiences remain underrepresented in public narratives [35]. This dynamic can contribute to an unfamiliarity with the outcomes of historical epidemics, preventing practitioners from recognizing Long COVID. The success of infectious disease prevention and the ensuing decline in public knowledge about epidemics encourages the development of fantastical theories on pandemic source and spread (eg, 5G cell phone signals caused COVID-19) [36]. Without a working knowledge of historical epidemics, communities will be continually unprepared for and surprised by the diffuse, entrenched social problems caused by epidemics—including debilitating health conditions—and the high costs and challenges of resolving those problems.

As historians like Einar Wigen demonstrate, understanding the varying temporalities driving epidemic ends requires that we study epidemics. Understanding that the medical fallout of viral pandemics does not end with the acute crisis allows practitioners and public health officials to consider new ways to support the well-being of survivors who remain ill or experience new chronic conditions. One way to shift cultural expectations around epidemic ends is education, including the education offered by medical schools.

Studying historical epidemics and their aftermaths could have direct clinical implications, guiding what physicians should expect among surviving patients. For example, following the outbreak of a novel virus, doctors are likely to observe a small percentage of patients who survive their initial infection and then fail to get well or experience emerging health problems or new chronic diseases in the weeks and months following their infection. This reality, well established in the study of historical epidemics, might be one of the only exceptions to the adage that physicians should not look for a zebra where they are likely to find a horse. While viruses like influenza and SARS-CoV-2 are common causes of illness (“horses”), their long-term effects—such as encephalitis or dysautonomia—are less expected (“zebras”) and can go unrecognized without knowledge of a historical precedent.

Lacking a context for zebra-sighting, new and experienced physicians face the daunting task of understanding and treating survivors without the benefit of hindsight. When COVID-19 emerged, a global pandemic resulting in quarantine, school closures, and other lifestyle changes had not occurred at a similar scale since the fall wave of influenza in 1918. Recent research suggests that medical students are only loosely acquainted with epidemic history, which is understandable given the amount of material students must learn. In the late aughts, medical students’ knowledge of influenza was insufficient; a quarter of the medical students in one study misidentified the transmission route, and more than 60 percent affirmed that drugs aimed to kill bacteria could treat flu cases [37].9 These educational gaps signify the importance of the history of medicine and epidemics as distinct units within required courses, additional coursework, or continuing education programs.

These lessons cannot come soon enough as the return of infectious diseases from early in the 20th century highlights the need for this education today. Unfortunately, current medical students might treat diseases that the last two generations of doctors have never observed in person. In February 2025, measles and tuberculosis are plaguing communities in multiple states around the country. The influenza strain H5N1 has circulated in wild bird populations for years—the recent spread into industrial avian settings brings the virus closer to a zoonotic leap. This threat is particularly alarming in a political context where some appointed officials do not support vaccination or the study of contagious diseases.

The danger we face without a detailed understanding of the long-term cultural, social, and medical consequences of pandemics is playing out now as Americans forget the lessons of COVID-19; a few deny the virus exists or existed. Americans remain uninformed about Long COVID, and few understand that it could be a pressing public health issue for the foreseeable future [38]. As we plan for future pandemics, we must rediscover and maintain the institutional and social flexibility we created in early 2020, when the world abruptly changed. Public health institutions must reintegrate the history of epidemics as well as epidemic response planning into medical education, community outreach, and patient support systems. How can clinics, research studies, and treatments be tailored to support patients who do not get well or experience new chronic conditions in the aftermath? What kinds of work and school accommodations can medical practitioners recommend? How might nutritionists, physical therapists, and mental health professionals offer care that reflects the unique needs of pandemic survivors with post-infection health conditions? These are all questions policy planners and institutions can be asking now, in response to COVID-19, but also as it pertains to future outbreaks.

Because our world maintains the dramaturgy of epidemics and pandemics in the wings, understanding the long and complex endings of outbreaks allows patients and practitioners to resist forgetting the lessons of the past. This question— how do we resist forgetting? —posed by patients and practitioners alike, reminds us that epidemic endings must be understood not only epidemiologically but experientially.

ack.p: Thank you to the Yale Journal of Biology and Medicine and reviewers for editorial feedback.

fn.p: 1 For more on pandemic narratives and public and media discourse see Albert [39] and Wald [40].

2 Throughout this section, I’m generalizing—there are more fissures and fault lines among and intersections between medical history, plague historians, and other epidemic historians. Within plague studies, the watershed finding that Yersinia pestis did cause the Black Death, or as it was termed “the Great Mortality,” completely changed the course of plague research. The original publication is Bos [41].

3 See Crosby [11]. This book was re-released as America’s Forgotten Pandemic: The Influenza of 1918 and is now in its 3rd edition. There is ongoing disagreement around the 1920 wave as we cannot confirm the genetic signature of the 1920 outbreak. However, given the multiple waves of COVID-19, it is not unreasonable to assume the 1920 outbreak, which was etiologically similar, was a direct descendant of the fall 1918 influenza strain (ie, as Omicron and Delta were COVID-19 strains).

4 The 1918 influenza pandemic strain spread throughout the world from the late summer of 1918 and through early 1919. The fall 1918 marks the acute wave or the epidemic crisis of the pandemic, though some historians and scientists today include the 1919 and 1920 winter waves in the pandemic. Bristow [42], Mamelund [43], and Siddharth [44].

5 Sergeant’s Mange Medicine advertisements regularly featured in southern newspapers, including The News Leader of Richmond. On June 27, 1919, p. 7 [45], the company used the same image in an advertisement titled “We the Barbers of Richmond,” which listed the names of barbers who used and endorsed the product. In “Can A Hair Root Smell?” published in The News Leader, August, 4 1919, p. 16 [46], the copy notes that that product helps “if you are losing your hair.”

6 Some doctors who posted on TikTok include Dr. Monica Kieu who posted in March 18, 2022 and cited telogen effluvium as a common side effect of COVID https://www.tiktok.com/@drfinlaysprivatepractice/video/7388045077102202144, and Dr. Finlay who posted through the account @dr.FinlaysPrivatePractice and shared that he lost a lot of his hair when he suffered from Long COVID: https://www.tiktok.com/@drfinlaysprivatepractice/video/7388045077102202144.
7 Throughout this section, I draw patient narratives from the collection by Ladd et al [28] and I rely on interviews I conducted with individuals with Long COVID.

8 As Callard and Perego argued in [33] “patients continue to experience epistemic injustice—their long-term symptoms misunderstood or reduced to anxiety.” Unlike patients dealing with the sequelae of influenza, COVID-19 “long haulers” were not taken seriously for some time ([33, p.113426])

9 In Herman et al. [37], researchers surveyed 69% of medical students at the medical school at the University of Alberta. Regarding treatment, 28 or 7.9% of students stated that antibiotics could treat influenza while 58.8% said that antibacterial drugs could treat the disease ([37, p.1781]) Antivirals can treat influenza if taken within 48 hours of symptom onset, and users experience one to two fewer days of symptomatic illness.”

notes.title: Author Contributions
notes.p: BLJ conceptualized and wrote the essay.

ref.mixed-citation: Ahsan S. In the Wake of the Coronavirus, Movies like Contagion and Outbreak Have Become Very Popular. [Internet] National Post. January 29, 2020. cited 2025 February 1. Available from: https://nationalpost.com/entertainment/movies/in-the-wake-of-the-coronavirus-movies-like-contagion-and-outbreak-have-become-very-popular
Charters E. Information, Expertise and Authority: The Many Ends of Epidemics. Centaurus. 2022;64(1):15–30. 10.1484/J.CNT.5.1301930008-8994
Berridge V. The Many Endings of Recent Epidemics: HIV/AIDS, Swine Flu 2009, and Policy. Centaurus. 2022;64(1):145–53. 10.1484/J.CNT.5.1294410008-8994
Wigen W. The Multiple Temporalities of Epidemic Endings. Centaurus. 2022;64(1):273–86. 10.1484/J.CNT.5.1299430008-8994
Green MH. The Four Black Deaths. The Am Hist Rev. 2020;125(5):1601-31. p. 1603. https://doi.org/10.1093/ahr/rhaa511.
Rosenberg CE. What is an Epidemic? AIDS in Historical Perspective. Bull Hist of Med. 2020;94(4):563-77. p. 564. https://dx.doi.org/https://doi.org/10.1353/bhm.2020.0082 10.1353/bhm.2020.008233775939
Barry D. Dickerson C. The Silent Killer of 1918: A Philadelphia Story. The New York Times. 2020 Apr. 5; Sec.A1, 1.

Wendorf KWinter KHarriman KZipprich JSchechter RHacker Jet al. Subacute Sclerosing Panencephalitis: the Devastating Measles Complication is More Common Than We Think. Open Forum Infect Dis. 2016;3 suppl 1:916. 10.1093/ofid/ofw194.812328-895728387784
Mystery. Evening Public Ledger. 1920, Feb. 18;8.
In-Flu-Enza and out-flew-hair. Richmond Times-Dispatch. 1919, Aug 12;12.
Crosby A. Epidemic and Peace, 1918. Westport: Greenwood; 1976. p. 53.
Russell F. A Journal of the Plague: The 1918 Influenza [Internet]. Yale Rev. 1958;47(2):223–4. [cited 2025 Feb 1] Available from: https://yalereview.org/article/journal-plague0044-0124
